title: C# Advanced — Paper 6
description: Advanced C# covering Delegates, Events, Async/Await, Task Parallel Library, Async Patterns & CancellationToken, Extension Methods, Expression Trees, Dynamic Types, Advanced Pattern Matching, and Records/Tuples/Deconstruction.

questions.text: What is CancellationToken and how is it used to cancel an async operation?

options.text: CancellationToken directly stops a thread when cancelled.

CancellationToken is a struct passed to async methods; the method periodically checks IsCancellationRequested or calls ThrowIfCancellationRequested(), and the caller cancels via CancellationTokenSource.Cancel().
CancellationToken automatically disposes resources when cancelled.
CancellationToken is only used with Parallel.For, not async/await.

questions.text: What is a record type in C# 9+ and what does it provide by default?

options.text: A record is a class with a private constructor.
A record is a reference type with compiler-generated value-based equality, immutable init-only properties, Deconstruct(), ToString(), and a 'with' expression for non-destructive mutation.
A record is a value type stored on the stack like a struct.
A record provides thread-safety for all its properties.

questions.text: What does a multicast delegate's invocation return when all methods return values?

options.text: It returns a collection of all return values.
Only the return value of the last method in the invocation list is returned to the caller; all earlier return values are discarded.
It throws an exception because multicast delegates cannot have return values.
It returns the first non-null return value.

questions.text: What are the differences between event and a public delegate field in C#?

options.text: They are identical; event is just an alias for a delegate field.

A public delegate field can be directly assigned (=), cleared (= null), and invoked from outside the class; an event restricts external code to only subscribe (+=) and unsubscribe (-=), preventing external invocation or replacement.
Events support multicast; delegate fields do not.
Delegate fields are thread-safe; events are not.

questions.text: What is ValueTask<T> and when should it be preferred over Task<T>?

options.text: ValueTask<T> is always faster and should replace Task<T> everywhere.

ValueTask<T> is a struct that avoids heap allocation when the result is already available synchronously (common in hot paths); use Task<T> for long-running operations that always complete asynchronously.
ValueTask<T> is used only for CPU-bound work.
ValueTask<T> does not support cancellation.

questions.text: What is IAsyncEnumerable<T> and how is it consumed?

options.text: IAsyncEnumerable<T> is a synchronous collection that runs on a background thread.
IAsyncEnumerable<T> represents an async stream of values; it is consumed with 'await foreach', which awaits each item as it becomes available without blocking the thread between items.
IAsyncEnumerable<T> requires manual Task.WhenAll() to enumerate.
IAsyncEnumerable<T> materializes all values before enumeration begins.

questions.text: What happens when you use AsParallel().WithDegreeOfParallelism(n) in PLINQ?

options.text: It creates exactly n dedicated threads.
It limits PLINQ to use at most n concurrent tasks from the thread pool, balancing parallelism with resource constraints.
It forces sequential execution with n as a batch size.
It sets the maximum number of elements to process.

questions.text: What is the 'with' expression in C# records and how does it work?

options.text: It modifies the original record in place.
It creates a copy of a record with specified properties changed and all other properties preserved from the original, enabling non-destructive mutation of immutable records.
It is a using-like syntax for resource management in records.
It merges two records into one.

questions.text: How do you implement an extension method that works on IQueryable<T> for a custom filter?

options.text: Extension methods cannot target IQueryable<T>.

Define a static method in a static class taking 'this IQueryable<T> source' and an Expression<Func<T,bool>> predicate, calling source.Where(predicate) and returning IQueryable<T> to preserve query composition.
Use a Func<T,bool> parameter instead of Expression<Func<T,bool>> for IQueryable.
Call .AsEnumerable() first, then apply the extension method.

questions.text: What is an async stream (C# 8+) and how do you produce one?

options.text: An async stream is produced by returning Task<IEnumerable<T>>.
An async stream is produced by an async iterator method that returns IAsyncEnumerable<T> and uses 'yield return' with 'await' to asynchronously produce each value.
Async streams require a background Task that pushes values to a queue.
Async streams are produced by calling Observable.Create() in Rx.

questions.text: What is a value tuple in C# and how does it differ from System.Tuple?

options.text: Value tuples and System.Tuple are identical.

Value tuples (System.ValueTuple) are value types (structs) with named elements, supporting deconstruction and more concise syntax; System.Tuple is a reference type with positional Item1/Item2 properties and no named element support.
Value tuples are allocated on the heap; System.Tuple is on the stack.
System.Tuple supports named elements; value tuples do not.

questions.text: What is the ExpressionVisitor base class used for in C#?

options.text: It is used to traverse the file system like a directory visitor.
ExpressionVisitor provides a base class for walking and optionally modifying expression trees; override specific Visit* methods to intercept and rewrite nodes of interest.
It is only useful for compiling expressions, not traversing them.
ExpressionVisitor executes expression trees without compiling.

questions.text: What does the 'dynamic' keyword NOT give you compared to using 'object'?

options.text: dynamic gives no access to any properties.

dynamic bypasses compile-time type checking entirely; you lose IntelliSense, compiler errors for typos in member names, and static analysis, unlike 'object' which still enforces compile-time typing before casting.
dynamic is slower than 'object' in every situation.
dynamic cannot call methods; it only accesses fields.

questions.text: What does deconstruction mean for value tuples in C#?

options.text: It converts a tuple to a string representation.
Deconstruction unpacks a tuple (or any type with a Deconstruct method) into individual named variables: var (name, age) = person; assigns the components to name and age directly.
Deconstruction removes elements from a tuple permanently.
Deconstruction is only available for System.Tuple, not value tuples.

questions.text: What is the 'relational' pattern in C# 9+ pattern matching?

options.text: A pattern that checks object identity via ReferenceEquals.
A pattern using comparison operators (< > <= >=) directly in a pattern: e.g., 'x is >= 0 and <= 100' matches when x is in the range [0, 100].
A pattern that compares two variables for equality.
A pattern that matches database relation objects.

questions.text: How does PLINQ handle ordering and what option preserves it?

options.text: PLINQ always preserves order automatically.
PLINQ does not preserve order by default for performance; call .AsOrdered() to request order preservation, though this introduces synchronization overhead.
PLINQ preserves order only when using OrderBy.
PLINQ order is controlled by thread scheduling and cannot be configured.

questions.text: What is a record struct (C# 10+) and how does it differ from a record class?

options.text: Record struct and record class are identical.

A record struct is a value type (allocated on the stack) with compiler-generated value equality and Deconstruct(); a record class is a reference type. Record structs are mutable by default unless declared readonly.
Record structs cannot have 'with' expressions.
Record structs are sealed; record classes are not.

questions.text: What is the purpose of CancellationTokenSource.CreateLinkedTokenSource()?

options.text: It creates a new token that is independent of all parent tokens.
It creates a CancellationTokenSource whose token is cancelled when any of the provided tokens is cancelled, enabling composite cancellation from multiple sources.
It merges two tokens into a single token that requires both to be cancelled.
It clones a CancellationToken for use in a child task.

questions.text: What is the difference between Task.Delay() and Thread.Sleep() in async contexts?

options.text: They are interchangeable in async methods.
await Task.Delay() suspends the async method without blocking the thread, freeing it for other work; Thread.Sleep() blocks the thread for the duration, preventing it from handling other requests.
Task.Delay() is less precise than Thread.Sleep().
Thread.Sleep() is preferred in async methods for simplicity.

questions.text: How can you implement Deconstruct() on a custom class to enable tuple-style assignment?

options.text: Implement the IDeconstructable interface with a Deconstruct method.
Define a public void Deconstruct(out T1 a, out T2 b, ...) method on the class; the compiler recognizes it and allows: var (a, b) = myObject; syntax.
Mark the class with [Deconstruct] attribute.
Override the implicit conversion operator to ValueTuple.

questions.text: How does event thread-safety work with the Interlocked class for event subscriptions?

options.text: Events are always thread-safe without any explicit synchronization.

Since delegates are immutable in .NET, subscribing/unsubscribing atomically replaces the backing delegate field; Interlocked.CompareExchange can be used in custom add/remove accessors to implement lock-free, thread-safe event management.
Interlocked cannot be used with delegates.
Thread safety for events requires a ReaderWriterLockSlim.

questions.text: What does the 'switch expression' (C# 8+) enable that switch statements do not?

options.text: Switch expressions support fall-through between arms.

Switch expressions are value-producing: they return a value from each arm, support exhaustiveness checking, use '=>' for concise arms, and integrate pattern matching more cleanly than switch statements.
Switch expressions require all cases to be constant values.
Switch expressions automatically handle null values.

questions.text: What is an anonymous type in C# and how is it related to tuples?

options.text: Anonymous types and tuples are identical in C#.

An anonymous type is a compiler-generated reference type created with 'new { Name = x, Age = y }' and is read-only; tuples are value types supporting deconstruction. Anonymous types are used in LINQ projections; tuples offer named access without a class definition.
Anonymous types support mutable properties; tuples are always immutable.
Anonymous types can be returned from public methods; tuples cannot.

questions.text: How does expression tree manipulation enable runtime code generation?

options.text: Expression trees can only be used for read-only code analysis.

You can build an expression tree programmatically at runtime using Expression.* factory methods, then call .Compile() to generate a delegate backed by IL code, effectively generating and executing code at runtime without Reflection.Emit.
Runtime code generation requires the Roslyn compiler API, not expression trees.
Expression tree compilation is only available in .NET Framework.

questions.text: What is ExpandoObject's relationship to IDictionary<string,object>?

options.text: ExpandoObject does not implement IDictionary.
ExpandoObject implements IDictionary<string,object>, so you can add/remove/iterate its dynamic properties via dictionary syntax: ((IDictionary<string,object>)expando)["Name"] = "Alice".
IDictionary<string,object> is only used for serialization of ExpandoObject.
Casting ExpandoObject to IDictionary throws an InvalidCastException.

questions.text: What happens to a CancellationToken after CancellationTokenSource.Cancel() is called?

options.text: The token is disposed and cannot be used again.
IsCancellationRequested becomes true, registered callbacks are invoked synchronously, and any awaiting ThrowIfCancellationRequested() calls will throw OperationCanceledException.
Existing async operations are immediately terminated.
The CancellationToken is reset and reusable.

questions.text: In PLINQ, what is the purpose of .ForAll() vs .ToList() when collecting results?

options.text: .ForAll() preserves order; .ToList() does not.

.ForAll() processes results in parallel without merging into a single collection; .ToList() merges all parallel results into a List<T> sequentially, introducing a merge overhead. .ForAll() is preferred when order and collection aren't needed.
.ForAll() is synchronous; .ToList() is asynchronous.
They are interchangeable with no performance difference.

questions.text: What is record inheritance in C# 9+ and what constraint applies?

options.text: Records cannot participate in inheritance.
A record can inherit from another record (not from a class); the derived record extends the base record's properties and the compiler-generated equality considers all properties from both.
Records support multiple inheritance.
Record inheritance automatically makes the derived record sealed.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the Task.Yield() method in async programming?

options.text: Task.Yield() returns the current result to the caller immediately.

Task.Yield() creates an awaitable that asynchronously yields control back to the current context, forcing the remainder of the method to run as a continuation, which can prevent stack overflows in async recursion and improve responsiveness.
Task.Yield() is equivalent to Thread.Yield() for thread scheduling.
Task.Yield() produces the next value in an async stream.

questions.text: How do you use a fluent extension method to build a complex pipeline (e.g., query builder)?

options.text: Each extension method must be in the same class as the type it extends.

Define extension methods on the builder type, each modifying and returning 'this' (or a new instance); callers chain them like: query.Where(...).OrderBy(...).Limit(10).Execute(), with each method adding to the query state.
Fluent pipelines require implementing the Builder interface.
Extension methods cannot be chained in a fluent pattern.
